coredata.dc:title: Xylan-based ratiometric fluorescence carbon dots composite with delignified wood for highly efficient water purification and photothermal conversion
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yingying
Xiao, Huining
Xiong, Ranhua
Huang, Chaobo
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel xylan derived carbon dots (XMR-CDs) used as mercury ion (Hg2+) ratiometric fluorescence sensor via amidation reaction was firstly prepared. And by impregnating 0D XMR-CDs into 3D balsa delignified wood (DW) network for construction porous fluorescent wood for simultaneous detection and adsorption of Hg2+ (XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ ). XMR-CDs exhibited rapid fluorescene response time and low detection limits (LOQs) of 1.7 nM towarded Hg2+ with F 580 nm/F 436 nm = 3.39 + 0.02[Hg2+], R2 = 0.9856. And the recoveries of XMR-CDs detection Hg2+ in real water and food samples were in the range of 97.8–104.8%. Adsorption experiments confirmed that XMR-CDs-DW displayed adsorption capacity of Hg2+ over 291.4 mg g−1. Further, XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ was covered into a suitable material (XMR-CDs-DW-HgS) by using in-situ sulfurization method for photothermal conversion, which exhibiting a excellent photothermal conversion rate of 3.3895 kg m-2h−1. Importanly, sea water purified by XMR-CDs-DW-HgS could also be utilized for real-time to cultivate plant and zebrafish, which proving the feasibility and potential of solar desalination–cultivation integrated system. This work highlights a strategy for Hg2+ detection, adsorption and removal, and reuse hazardous waste for photothermal evaporation, which providing practical applications for water purification and environmental protection.

head.ce:title: Xylan-based ratiometric fluorescence carbon dots composite with delignified wood for highly efficient water purification and photothermal conversion

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel xylan derived carbon dots (XMR-CDs) used as mercury ion (Hg2+) ratiometric fluorescence sensor via amidation reaction was firstly prepared. And by impregnating 0D XMR-CDs into 3D balsa delignified wood (DW) network for construction porous fluorescent wood for simultaneous detection and adsorption of Hg2+ (XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ ). XMR-CDs exhibited rapid fluorescene response time and low detection limits (LOQs) of 1.7 nM towarded Hg2+ with F 580 nm/F 436 nm = 3.39 + 0.02[Hg2+], R2 = 0.9856. And the recoveries of XMR-CDs detection Hg2+ in real water and food samples were in the range of 97.8–104.8%. Adsorption experiments confirmed that XMR-CDs-DW displayed adsorption capacity of Hg2+ over 291.4 mg g−1. Further, XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ was covered into a suitable material (XMR-CDs-DW-HgS) by using in-situ sulfurization method for photothermal conversion, which exhibiting a excellent photothermal conversion rate of 3.3895 kg m-2h−1. Importanly, sea water purified by XMR-CDs-DW-HgS could also be utilized for real-time to cultivate plant and zebrafish, which proving the feasibility and potential of solar desalination–cultivation integrated system. This work highlights a strategy for Hg2+ detection, adsorption and removal, and reuse hazardous waste for photothermal evaporation, which providing practical applications for water purification and environmental protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hg2+ as one of the most toxic elements that is commonly found in water and soil [1,2]. Various organizations have set standards for the maximum allowable levels of Hg2+ in wastewater and drinking water [3,4]. The Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) mandates that Hg2+ content in real samples should not exceed 0.02 mg/kg. The World Health Organization (WHO) has recommended that Hg2+ concentration in drinking water should not exceed 2 μg L-1, while the Ministry of Ecology and Environment of China mandates that Hg2+ content in domestic water should not exceed 0.001 mg/L [5]. Additionally, European Union has set the upper limit for Hg2+ in drinking water at 1.0 μg/L [6]. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has set the upper limit for Hg2+ concentration in drinking water at 2 μg/L [7]. The enrichment of Hg2+ in food chain can pose a significant risk to both the ecological environment and human health. Hence, efficient materials are urgently needed for the detection, adsorption, and removal of Hg2+ from wastewate.

Compared to common analytical techniques such as atomic adsorption spectroscopy (AAS), atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (AFS), and inductively-coupled plasma emission spectroscopy (ICP-ES), fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have drawn much attention in Hg2+ detection due to excellent water solubility, non-toxicity and easy functionalization [8,42]. Hamarawf et al., developed a N-doped carbon quantum dots for detection Hg2+ with a detection limit of 10.0 nM in environmental applications [3]. Song et al., developed a fluorescence CDs from carbonaceous waste for Hg2+ detection in lake water and cosmetic, demonstrating their potential towards diverse applications [9]. Yang et al., developed a Si/CDs material via a conventional solvothermal method for Hg2+ detection with a detection limit of 26.7 nM in water environment [10]. In particular, biomass fluorescent CDs for Hg2+ detection have been exploited owing excellent biocompatibility [11]. Among them, xylan as a kind of biomass materials can be hydrothermal carbonized at low temperature, which not only reduces the requirements for reaction equipment, and effectively avoid the overuse of chemical reagents and environmental pollution caused by it [12,44]. Yang et al., [13] designed a xylan-derived CDs for detection of tetracycline with detection limit of 6.49 nM. Cai et al., [14] reported a single-layered single-layered graphene quantum dots (GQDs) fabricated by nonaromatic xylan for visual detection of trace chromium(Vl) with with a detection limit of 0.10 μM. Both of the researches proved the feasibility of preparing fluorescent CDs using xylan as substrate. Besides, to improve stability of material for Hg2+ detection and adsorption capacity, the number of binding sites and adsorption capacities can be increased by self-assembly of CDs and a solid matrix with a three-dimensional network structure.

Delignified wood (DW) could chelating sites for heavy Hg2+ binding due to its high adsorption capacity, three-dimensional (3D) porous structure, and abundant functional groups [15,16]. Currently, there are few literature of fluorescent wood combined with biomass-CDs recognition and adsorption of Hg2+. Therefore, it is imperative to upcycle these discarded materials into valuable products. A method to reuse these materials is to use them to absorb solar radiation and generate vapor for desalination. Under the action of capillary suction, it can continuously transport water from the bottom upwards[15,43]. In addition, the low thermal conductivity of wood can effectively prevent heat loss to ensure evaporation efficiency [17].

Xylan (from birch, 95%), sodium chlorite (25% in H2O), glacial acetic acid (99.5%), melamine (99%), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 95%), rhodamine B (RhB), mercuric sulfate (99%), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N'-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride, EDC, 98.5%) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%) were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd, respectively. Balsa wood was bought from online shop. Anhydrous ethanol (EtOH, 95%) was purchased from reagent store. Regenerated cellulose membrane tubing bags (Mw = 500 Da and 300 Da) were bought from online shop. Preparation of simulated seawater: 1.0 g sodium chloride, 0.037 g potassium chloride, 0.05 g calcium chloride, 0.305 g magnesium chloride hexahydrate and 0.059 g magnesium sulfate were dissolved in 50 mL ultra-pure water and mixed well, respectively. Real sea water was taken from the Yellow Sea of Qingdao, China (119°30′E, 35°35′N).

XM-CDs (30 mg) was dissolved in 15 mL of anhydrous ethanol (95%) and slowly stirred at room temperature for 10 min. Besides, 0.07 mmol EDC and 0.16 mmol NHS solution were added to a beek of a certain volume. Above solution were evenly mixed and added with 7.8 mg RhB solution. After agitated at room temperature for 24 h, the filtrate was dialyzed for 48 h with cellulose dialysis membrane (300 Da) to remove the filtrate as reaction reagent. The dialysate was changed every 12 h during dialysis. The solution in the dialysis bag was collected and dried at 60℃ for 12 h before the solids were collected, namely XMR-CDs.

XMR-CDs were mixed with different common substances (20 μM, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Ni2+, F-, CN–, HPO4 2-, NO2 –, SCN- and HS-) to test the selectively. Fluorescence spectrum change was recorded in the wavelength range of 350–700 nm with an excitation wavelength of 380 nm.
The related preparation process can be found in Supporting information.
The related preparation process can be found in Supporting information.

XMR-CDs-DW was placed in a solution containing 50 mL of mercury sulfate (100 mg L-1) and oscillated at 293 K in a water bath oscillator for 2 h. After adsorption equilibrium, the Hg2+ solution was filtered using a pinhole filter (0.45 μm), and 3 mL filtrate was collected and the concentration of Hg2+ was further determined by atomic fluorescence photometer (AFS-933). The effects of pH (1.0–5.0), contact time (0–200 min), initial concentration of Hg2+ (50–500 mg L-1) and temperature (293 K, 313 K, 333 K) on the adsorption properties of XMR-CDs-DW were investigated. The removal efficiency (R e, %) and adsorption efficiency (Q e, %) of Hg2+ by XMR-CDs-DW were determined based on the following formula:

In order to understand the adsorption mechanism of XMR-CDs-DW on Hg2+, pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models were used to analyze experimental data. The formulas are as follows:

Water evaporation experiments of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS were conducted in a crystallizing dish (100 mL) with distilled water (50 mL) that contained a foam and then irradiated under a solar simulator at room temperature. Mass changes of the system was measured on an electronic balance. Temperature change of surface of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS was recorded by using a infrared camera. The evaporation experiment was conducted under environmental temperature of ∼26 °C and relative humidity of ∼40%. Energy efficiency (η) can be obtained according following equation [18,19]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of biomass based fluorescent carbon dots (CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, biomass based CDs were prepared by a simple one-step hydrothermal synthesis method by using xylan as carbon source and melamine as a dopant (Fig. 2 ). It can be seen from Fig. 2 B that XM-CDs were monodisperse spherical particles at room temperature, and agglomeration did not occur. Fig. 2 C showed HRTEM experiment results. It can be observed that XM-CDs has significant lattice fringes. According to the selection diffraction results, the spacing between the graphitized carbon core layers was 0.21 nm, which consistent with the (100) crystal plane of the graphite phase. The results showed that the particle size of XM-CDs ranged from 1.7 nm to 3.25 nm (Fig. 2 D), indicating that XM-CDs had a wide particle size distribution range. In addition, the average particle size of XM-CDs was about 2.5 nm. AFM method (Fig. 2 E) indicated that XM-CDs were dispersed uniformly with a height of 2.9 nm. FTIR method showed that XM-CDs has a strong infrared absorption band near 3420 cm−1, indicating that the chemical composition contains abundant hydroxyl groups (Fig. 2 F). The weak absorption peak near 3247 cm−1 indicated that surface of XM-CDs contained a small amount of –NH2 groups. The absorption peak of 2925 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C–H. The wide absorption peak near 1600 cm−1 corresponded to stretching vibration of CC and bending vibration of N–H, and the absorption peak near 1407 cm−1 was related to stretching vibration of C–N. The characteristic absorption peak at 1068 cm−1 was the C–O stretching vibration absorption peak. The XRD pattern of XM-CDs (Fig. 2 G) had an apparent peak at approximately 25°, which was attributed to an interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm, while the peak near 41° represented 0.21 nm interlayer spacing. The full XPS spectrum presented in Fig. 2 H showed three peaks at 284.81 ev, 399.62 ev and 532.22 ev, suggesting that XM-CDs was consisted of C, N and O elements. In Fig. 2 I, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s band was separated into three peaks at 284.1 ev, 285. 1 ev, and 285.8 eV, which were assigned to C–C/CC, C–N and C-O, respectively. The O 1s band (Fig. 2 J) displayed two peaks at 531.5 ev and 532.3 eV, respectively, which correspond to CO and C-O-C/C-OH. And N 1s band was separated into three peaks at 398.6 ev for C-N, 399.07 ev for NH2 and 399.3 ev for –NH-/–NH2 (Fig. 2 K). These three high resolutions spectra collectively indicated successful insertion of N atoms into the XM-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of FRET ratiometric fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: According previous studies, it is found that the use of a single fluorescence signal could lead to sensing efficiency reduced, which has a certain impact on the detection accuracy of metal ions [20,21]. In order to solve this problem, Herein, biomass XM-CDs used as energy donor and RhB as energy acceptor through amide bond connection for conduction FRET system. The results were shown in Fig. 3 A. XM-CDs has a maximum absorption wavelength at 300 nm in Fig. 3B, and a maximum emission wavelength at 467 nm, which displayed a large Stokes shift value of 167 nm. Importantly, the XM-CDs solution showed light yellow under sunlight and bright blue fluorescence signal under ultraviolet lamp radiation, which further proved that XM-CDs has fluorescence properties, as shown in Fig. 3 B inset. Then, the excitation characteristics of XM-CDs was investigated. The results showed that the maximum emission wavelength at 467 nm of XM-CDs didn’t change with the change of excitation wavelength (from 252 nm to 382 nm), which has obvious excitation independence behavior, as shown in Fig. 3 C. Further, the response behavior of XM-CDs with Hg2+ (Fig. 3 D) was studied. The results showed that fluorescence intensity of XM-CDs at 467 nm showed a step decline and ‘turn-off’ singal with the increase of Hg2+concentration (F 467 nm = 417186.36–4316.63[Hg2+], R2 = 0.957, [Hg2+] = 5.0–14.0 μM), which proved that XM-CDs have high selectivity and excellent recognition performance for Hg2+. In addition, bright blue fluorescence signal of XM-CDs solution was quenched obviously after addition of Hg2+ (Fig. 3 D inset). Furthermore, in order to demonstrate the successful construction of the FRET system and its ability to produce a more precise response to Hg2+, the behavior of RhB in response to Hg2+ was first studied. The results showed that fluorescence intensity of RhB solution at 580 nm increased with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, showing an ‘turn-on’ signal (Fig. 3 E). Besides, the fluorescence signal of RhB solution was also enhanced with the addition of Hg2+ (F 580 nm = 355290.75 + 4337.59[Hg2+], R2 = 0.998, [Hg2+] = 2.0–10.0 μM) (Fig. 3 E inset). Then the response of XMR-CDs to common substances (20 μM, 1: control, 2: K+, 3: Ca2+, 4: Mg2+, 5: Zn2+, 6: Hg2+, 7: Fe2+, 8: Cu2+, 9: Al3+, 10: Fe3+, 11: Cr3+, 12: Ni2+, 13: F-, 14: CN–, 15: HPO4 2-, 16: NO2 –, 17: SCN- and 18: HS-) was studied and the anti-interference ability of detecting specific substance. As can be seen from the Fig. 3 F, when XMR-CDs was mixed with different substances, the ratio of fluorescence intensity (F 580 nm/F 436 nm) does not change significantly, but when XMR-CDs was mixed with Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity (F 580 nm/F 436 nm) of XMR-CDs solution was significantly enhanced, which proved that XMR-CDs have a more obvious response and detection ability to Hg2+. Further, compared to control group, there were no significant changes of fluorescence intensity ratio (F 580 nm/F 436 nm) of XMR-CDs in the presence of substances, indicating that XMR-CDs has great potential for precisely sensing Hg2+ variation with negligible interference. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of FRET ratio systems towarded Hg2+ (XMR-CDs) in Fig. 3 G showed that the maximum fluorescence intensity at 436 nm was quenched when different concentrations of Hg2+ were added into XMR-CDs solution, while the fluorescence intensity at 580 nm was significantly enhanced, showing an obvious ratio sensing behavior. The fluorescence intensity ratio at 580 nm and 436 nm also had a good linear relationship of detection limit of 1.7 nM (F 580 nm/F 436 nm = 3.39 + 0.02 [Hg2+], R2 = 0.9856, [Hg2+] = 2.0–10.0 μM), which further proved that XMR-CDs could produce high responsiveness to Hg2+ (Table S1). This phenomenon was taken as evidence that the observed Hg2+ induced fluorescence enhancing was due to interaction of Hg2+ with XMR-CDs. The fluorescence lifetime of XMR-CDs was about 1236 ns, which shifted to 1237 ns after introducing Hg2+ (Fig. 3 H and I).[22] Besides, under daylight conditions, XMR-CDs solution was light pink, and the solution produced bright orange fluorescence signal under ultraviolet lamp (365 nm), which further proved that the XMR-CDs has excellent fluorescence properties (Fig. 3 H inset). Importantly, When Hg2+ was added to the XMR-CDs solution, XMR-CDs solution appeared dark pink in sunlight and showed a pink fluorescent signal under an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm) (Fig. 3 I inset). Above color changed indicated that XMR-CDs can be used to detect Hg2+ by colorimetric method with naked-eyes. Then the time and fluorescence stability of Hg2+ detected by XMR-CDs was studied. The results showed that there was no significant change in the fluorescence intensity (F 580 nm/F 436 nm) of the XMR-CDs and XMR-CDs + Hg2+ within 12 days, which proved that XMR-CDs has good stability for detection Hg2+. Besides, In order to validate the feasibility of the fluorescence materials XMR-CDs for assay of Hg2+, XMR-CDs was employed to monitor Hg2+ in environment (tap water, bottle water, xuanwu Lake, soil) and food (fish, shrimp) samples by standard addition method. The recoveries of XMR-CDs during the detection of Hg2+ are in the range of 97.8–104.8% (Table S2), indicating the high accuracy and reliability of this fluorescence sensing system for Hg2+ detection in real samples. These results indicated that XMR-CDs fluorescent sensors were suitable for the determination of Hg2+ in environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of FRET ratiometric fluorescence wood
ce:section.ce:para:
Herein, a FRET ratiometric fluorescent wood containing XMR-CDs was prepared for absorption and removal of Hg2+. XMR-CDs-DW was fabricated by immersing the delignificated wood (DW) into XMR-CDs solution, as shown in Fig. 4
A. Fig. 4 B (a-f) showed macro-morphology of wood, DW, XMR-CDs-DW, XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ , respectively. Owning removal of chromophoric groups from the wood (Fig. 4 B(a)), the DW turned completely white (Fig. 4 B(b)). After decoration with XMR-CDs, XMR-CDs-DW featured a pink color under natural light (Fig. 4 B(c)) and orange color (Fig. 4 B(d)) under Uv–vis light indicating existence of fluorescence XMR-CDs. And after decoration XMR-CDs with Hg2+, XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ displayed pale color under natural light (Fig. 4 B(e)) and bright orange-red under Uv–vis light (Fig. 4 B(f)) indicating XMR-CDs-DW were successfully incorporated with Hg2+. Then, TEM indicated that XMR-CDs were well-dispersed with a diameter of about 3.4 nm (Fig. 4 B(g)). And in the presence of Hg2+, TEM image indicated that XMR-CD + Hg2+ with a diameter of about 4.1 nm (Fig. 4 B(h)), this result further indicated XMR-CDs could be used for Hg2+ sensing and removal. SEM was used to determine the morphology of DW, XMR-CDs-DW and XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ , respectively(Fig. 4 B(i-k)). Both of them exhibited a 3D porous network structure. These 3D network structures could promote adsorption of metal ions due to abundance of active sites dispersed throughout the XMR-CDs-DW, thus improving the adsorption ability. Moreover, FTIR (Fig. 4 C) spectra also suggested the effective incorporation between XMR-CDs-DW with Hg2+. Besides, compared with wood, DW, XMR-CDs-DW and XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ showed much faster water adsorption efficiency due to their hydrophilic improvements, as shown in (Fig. 4 Da-d). Further, XPS method proved that XMR-CDs-DW can be incorporation with Hg2+. In C 1 s, the peaks at 288.9 ev, 286.4 ev, 285.6 ev and 284.8 eV were assigned to CO, C-N, C-O and C–C/CC (Fig. 4 E), which correspond to oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional group of XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ in adsorption of Hg2+. The O 1 s showed three components related to C-O-C at 533.5 ev, C-OH at 532.7 ev and CO at 531.9 ev, which contributing to the metal ion adsorption (Fig. 4 F). Besides, N 1s showed two components related to NH at 399.2 ev and C-N at 400.9 ev, contributing to the following adsorption properties (Fig. 4 G). The Hg 4f of XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ could be divided into two peaks at 105.3 ev and 101.6 eV, which was ascribed to the Hg 4f5/2 and Hg 4f7/2 (Fig. 4 H). This phenomenon clearly revealed the adsorption of Hg2+ by using XMR-CDs-DW. The above investigation clearly demonstrated that XMR-CDs-DW has great potential for synergistic detection and removal of Hg2+.

The construction of adsorption strategy of 3D fluorescent XMR-CDs-DW for Hg2+ was shown in Fig. 5
A. When Hg2+ solution co-cultured with XMR-CDs-DW, Hg2+ undergo specific binding with the thiol group. Moreover, the porous 3D structure of XMR-CDs-DW provided Hg2+ transport channels, and promoted the diffusion of Hg2+ from the external surface of the XMR-CDs-DW to the interior. As such Hg2+ was adsorbed by the internal regions of the XMR-CDs-DW, and bound to carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of XMR-CDs-DW. Therefore, XMR-CDs-DW displayed excellent adsorption capacity for Hg2+. Subsequently, the adsorption properties of XMR-CDs-DW to obtain the best conditions for removing Hg2+ were evaluated. Owning pH is a significant factor for effective Hg2+ adsorption. The effect of pH on the adsorption ability of XMR-CDs-DW with Hg2+ was thoroughly evaluated first over a pH range from 1.0 to 5.0. The adsorption ability of Hg2+ by XMR-CDs-DW increased with increasing pH and reached the maximum at pH 5.0 (Fig. 5 B). Such behavior can be attributed to the enhanced electrostatic interactions between the XMR-CDs-DW and Hg2+. When pH > 3, removal efficiency Hg2+ was increased. However, at low pH (pH < 3), amino groups of XMR-CDs-DW was protonated and as such are positively charged, thus reducing the adsorption capacity. At very low pH < 2, the electro neutral HgSO4 was dominant. This neutral form does not support effective electrostatic interactions, thus reducing the Hg2+ removal efficiency. Therefore, pH 5 was chosen for subsequent experiments. To analyze the system in greater detail, a kinetic study was carried out. As shown in Fig. 5 C, the adsorption of Hg2+ on wood increased rapidly at first and then flattens out until equilibrium was reached about 1 h later. It was found that a pseudo-second-order model can be used to fit the experimental data with correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.9918 (Fig. 5 C inset). The suitability of the second order rate equation indicated that the chemical adsorption dominateed the adsorption process fof Hg2+. To further evaluate the adsorption properties, the effect of temperature on XMR-CDs-DW was studied, as shown in Fig. 5 D. The adsorption capacity increased with temperature increasing from 293 K to 333 K. The maximum adsorption capacity increased from 253 mg g−1 to 400 mg g−1, indicating that an increase of temperature was conducive to adsorption of Hg2+. Then, isotherm model was used to understand interaction between adsorbent and adsorption carrier. The adsorption capacity increased with the increase of concentration and then tends to saturation. The data fitted to the Freundlich and Langmuir models were conducted as shown in Fig. 5 E-G. The R2 value for the Freundlich model (R2 = 0.989) proved higher than Langmuir mode (R2 = 0.974) at 333 K. Fitting results were consistent with a heterogeneous adsorption process with multilayer adsorption. XMR-CDs-DW exhibited a good adsorption performance for Hg2+ with adsorption capacities of 291.4 mg g−1. Adsorption–desorption cycle experiments were carried out to determine recyclability of XMR-CDs-DW by regeneration of XMR-CDs-DW through treatment with HCl (1 M) solution for 2 h. As shown in Fig. 5 H, after eight desorption cycles, the Hg2+ adsorption behavior of XMR-CDs-DW did not noticeably change during repeated adsorption–desorption cycles, which exhibited good regenerative adsorption capability. The adsorption capacity of XMR-CDs-DW decreased to about 81.3 mg g−1 after eight cycles. The reduction of adsorption capacity can be attributed to the formation of irreversible Hg2+ complex with the XMR-CDs-DW. Further, in addition to Hg2+, the adsorption capacity of XMR-CDs-DW to Cu2+ and Cr3+ was also investigated, as shown in Fig. 5 I. The system exhibited a good adsorption performance for Cu2+, Cr3+ and Hg2+ with adsorption capacities of 50.4 mg g−1, 163.5 mg g−1 and 276.9 mg g−1, respectively. Therefore, XMR-CDs-DW can be employed for removal of metal ions in real samples. Besides, it was found that removal efficiency for various concentrations of Hg2+ was greater than 90% after treating with XMR-CDs-DW, as shown in Fig. 5 J. Further, it is important to observed that XMR-CDs-DW exhibited excellent Hg2+ removal efficiency over 0–120 min timeframe (Fig. 5 H).Then, SEM images displayed 3D porous structure of XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ ( Figure. S2 ). While for EDS mapping, Hg2+ was tightly bonded to XMR-CDs-DW surface and porous, which further confirmed that Hg2+ were adsorbed by the XMR-CDs-DW successfully. Above all, XMR-CDs-DW displayed better detection and recycle capabilities.

XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ not only exhibited good ability to detect, remove various Hg2+ but provide an opportunity for use in photothermal conversion. Herein, a simple strategy for converting waste adsorbents (XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ ) into materials for water treatment using solar desalination through in-situ sulfurization was provided, as shown in Fig. 6
A. First, XMR-CDs-DW-HgS was synthesized by the vulcanization reaction between the saturated adsorbent containing Hg2+ and Na2S solution. The color of XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ changed to black after sulfurization process, which can be used as a ‘photothermal absorber’ to convert light into heat ( Figure. S1 ). Encouraged by these favorable properties, the potential of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS for photothermal conversion application was next evaluated. The temperature change at the water evaporation surface of water, DW, XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2 and XMR-CDs-DW-HgS was monitored using an infrared camera under one standard sun irradiation at wetted state, respectively (Fig. 6 B and C). The surface temperature of the XMR-CDs-DW-HgS increased rapidly from 27.1 °C to 37.5 °C within solar radiation for 30 min under wetted state, as shown in Fig. 6 B. XMR-CDs-DW-HgS exhibited a higher performance than XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ and DW, which may be attributed to better optical properties of black XMR-CDs-DW-HgS compared with the pale pink of XMR-CDs-DW-Hg2+ and white of DW. Above results showed that XMR-CDs-DW-HgS has an excellent heat localization effect, which could accelerate the conversion of light into heat and contribute to solar steam generation. IR images in Fig. 6 C also showed obvious temperature increase of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS under one standard sun irradiation. Water evaporated of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS shown in Fig. 6 D depiceted at a rate of 2.18 kg m-2h−1 under one sun irradiation, representing 1.8 fold enhancement over evaporation campared to 1. 21 kg m-2h−1 for DW. Besides, the dark evaporation was 0. 0142 kg m-2h−1. As a result, the practical water evaporation rate of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS was 3.3895 kg m-2h−1, which was a higher performance when compare to currenttly study (Fig. 6 E). And corresponding energy efficiency of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS was calculated as 89.1% under one sun illumination, which displayed great potential as a photothermal evaporator [23]. It is acknowledged that long-term performance stability is of paramount importance for practical applicability of the solar evaporation device. Further, as such from Fig. 6 F, the water evaporation mass change of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS remained negligible change after six cycles under one sun irradiation, implying great photothermal stability in water [24,25]. Owning XMR-CDs-DW-HgS has 3D opened pores and rough lumen of substrate, and displaying excellent hydrophilicity, light absorption property and outstanding photothermal conversion capacity, which could help enhance the light harvesting and multiple scattering properties [26]. It worth noted that the high-throughput natural channel of wood has a certain salt removal effect, effectively avoiding salt deposition, thus achieving recyclability of wood-based solar evaporators. Herein, to explore the salt rejection ability of the sample, XMR-CDs-DW-HgS was put on a salt solution (3.5 wt% as the simulated seawater, and RhB stained NaCl powders (1.0 g) were then spread on the surface of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS. Under one standard sun irradiation, NaCl powder gradually dissolved and completely dispared in the 3.5 wt% brine after 160 min (Fig. 6 G). The rapid dissolution of NaCl powder on the surface of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS may be related to uniformly distributed mesoporous channels within the XMR-CDs-DW-HgS. Due to being trapped by mesopores, salt ions do not accumulate extensively and can diffuse downwards along concentration gradients [27]. Next, the salt tolerance of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS and found that after 24 h of without sun radiation was examined, only a small number of salt particles appeared on the surface of the sample (Fig. 6 H), which can be attributed that under dark conditions, the evaporation rate of water decreases, causing the salt accumulated in the XMR-CDs-DW-HgS channels and pores to gradually dissolve [16], as shown in Fig. 6 I. All above results indicated that XMR-CDs-DW-HgS has remarkable salt tolerance and excellent self-desalting capacity, which could adequately guarantee the long-term durability of 3D mesoporous wood-based evaporator during the seawater desalination procedure [28].

The solar desalinization capability of 3D XMR-CDs-DW-HgS evaporator from complex seawater (Yellow Sea) by using a self-assembled experimental equipment was also tested, as shown in Fig. 7
A. The Yellow Sea water was used as real water sample and used XMR-CDs-DW-HgS to conduct solar evaporation experiments. The generated steam can continuously condense and naturally condense into water droplets on the inner walls of the top and sides, then accumulate into water flow and slide down the device wall, and can be collected for further application. Initially, by measuring the conductivity of condensed water, the efficiency of seawater purification can be evaluated. The conductivity of original Yellow Sea water was determined to be 8.21 mS cm−1. After one standard solar irradiation, collected distilled water had a conductivity of 289 μS cm−1 proved that XMR-CDs-DW-HgS also has excellent seawater desalination performance for real seawater, as shown in Fig. 7 A bottom. Furthermore, the performance of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS in desalination and organic pollutants (Rho-B and MB solution as samples) was also conducted. Importantly, the concentrations of primary ions (Na+, Ca2+, K+ and Mg2+) were also decreased from 11374 mg L−1, 287 mg L−1, 394 mg L−1 and 1365 mg L−1 to 24.1 mg L−1, 8.7, mg L−1, 39.5 mg L−1 and 13.7 mg L−1 after desalination (Table S3), respectively, which fitted the drinking water standard of the World Health Organization (WHO) [15,41]. Further purification ability of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS for evaporating complex solution was confirmed. Besides, the ability of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS to absorb organic pollutants for actual water purification has also been evaluated. The water purification quality was qualitatively assessed by using UV–Vis adsorption spectra. After RhB and MB solution were evaporated by a solar evaporation device of XMR-CDs-DW-HgS, the absorbance significantly decreases and the color becomed colorless, as shown in Fig. 7 B and C, indicating XMR-CDs-DW-HgS possessed a good adsorption efficiency. Importantly, calculated removal efficiency ( Supporting information ) of RhB and MB was greater than 97%. Hence, XMR-CDs-DW-HgS has displayed multifunction in solar thermal desalination and dye adsorption. For practical application, we conducted a study on the biocompatibility for plant and animal of the purified water sample (Fig. 7 D). Initially, the capability of the purified water to grow tabacco seeds was characterized with unpurified seawater and underground water as control groups to prove the plant compatibility [28]. The cultivated area for each water source was about 46.24 cm2 and corresponded of four tobacco seeds. As shown in Fig. 7 E, tobacco seeds irrigated with real seawater have exceeded the plant's needs due to high salt concentration, making them completely unable to take root and sprout. However, wheat irrigated with groundwater and purified seawater through desalination culture systems has better growth potential and can even bloom. Further, the growing speed of shoot length along with time displayed the same tendency of condensate seawater and underground water, indicating that the condensate seawater was crop-growing compatible and can satisfy the need for tobacco growth. Importantly, a simple device integrating solar evaporation-water collection-irrigation with foam panels was built to prove that the water collection system has practical application potential. As depicted in Fig. 7 E, after a sun radiation, the XMR-CDs-DW-HgS can generate condensed water and return to the foam channel, then flowed into the container containing Chinese cabbage seeds. After 24 h, Chinese cabbage seeds sprout and grow cotyledons. Then, zebrafish compatibility of the purified water was conducted through a real time and on demand solar desalination–cultivation system for zebrafish growing, as shown in Fig. 7 G. After 20 days of co-cultivation with purified water and zebrafish, zebrafish still display survived, proving that zebrafish cultured in desalination–cultivation has good biocompatibility. Hence, this approach provides a new strategy for the reuse of hazardous waste, thus reducing environmental pollution. Above results indicate that XMR-CDs-DW-HgS has the possibility of scale-up production, and proving the feasibility and potential of desalination–cultivation integrated system.

At present, the research on Hg2+ detection/adsorption materials in the field of water purification mainly focuses on their adsorption or detection performance, resulting in a single function and poor universality. Moreover, the materials that adsorb Hg2+ have not been properly recycled and utilized, which undoubtedly has an impact on environment. Herein, xylan-based fluorescence CDs composited with porous wood for Hg2+ sensing, detection, absorption and repurposed (upcycled) to generate solar water vapor used for plant and zebrafish cultivation was reported, which providing practical applications for environmental protection. In addition, the preparation methods and materials selected in this study have great flexibility, providing the possibility for large-scale low-cost production of biomass resource detection adsorption materials. This design strategy is expected to provide a new concept for the construction of biocompatible adsorption materials for water purification and promoting practical application of multifunctional integrated adsorption materials.
